summary: DeVault shares practical advice on making a living from free and open-source software, drawing from his own experience running SourceHut. He divides the landscape into two paths: getting employed by companies that invest in FOSS, or building your own FOSS business. For employment, he offers a clever git log trick to identify companies contributing to projects you care about. For building a business, he warns against venture capital culture and emphasizes treating your project as a real business with sustainable revenue streams like SaaS hosting and consulting.
key_points: You can identify potential FOSS employers by analyzing git commit email domains in repositories you want to work on
Donations alone rarely sustain a living — programmers who live off donations are a small minority
Venture capital culture is stressful, unlikely to succeed, and socially irresponsible — sustainable FOSS businesses require patience and planning
SourceHut's model combines hosted SaaS (no free tier) with free software consulting on any open-source project
Making money from your own FOSS work requires developing genuine business skills and embracing your inner suit
money_quotes: 100% of my code is free and open-source. There's no proprietary add-ons, no periodic code dumps, just 100% bona-fide free and open source software.
You're unlikely to make a living from donations. Programmers who do are a small minority.
The toxic startup culture around venture capital and endless hyper-growth is more stressful, less likely to succeed, and socially irresponsible.
The fast route — venture capital funded — is going to impose constraints on your business that will ultimately make it difficult to remain true to your open-source mission.
And yes, you are building a business. You need to start thinking of your project as a business and of yourself as a business owner.
At times, you will be forced to embrace your inner suit.
Remember to turn that engineering mind of yours towards the task of monetization, and experiment with and invent new ways of making money that best suit the kind of software you want to work on.
key_insight: Making a sustainable living from FOSS requires either strategically targeting companies that invest in open source, or building a real business with engineering-minded pragmatism while rejecting venture capital culture.
